text: The surgeon approaches the thymoma from the left side using thoracoscopy, with the patient positioned supine on the operating table.
The surgeon utilizes three thoracoscopic ports: two for surgical instruments and one for the camera, with an optional fourth port for tissue retraction during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon visualizes the phrenic nerve alongside the thymic tumor, highlighting the anatomical proximity of these structures during the procedure.

Carbon dioxide gas is insufflated into the thoracic cavity at a pressure of nine to ten millimeters of mercury, aiding in the visualization and manipulation of the thymic tumor during the thoracoscopic thymectomy.
The surgeon begins adhesiolysis to separate adhesions between the thymic tumor and the lung tissue, using thoracoscopic instruments to carefully dissect these connections.
The surgeon carefully dissects the parietal pleura over the mediastinum, located just anterior to the phrenic nerve, taking precautions to avoid thermal injury to the nerve.
The surgeon makes an anterior cut along the anterior reflection of the pleura, with the thymic tumor visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses carbon dioxide insufflation to drop the anterior mediastinal contents towards the specimen, facilitating exposure and manipulation during the thymectomy.
The surgeon carefully secures the thymic vessels, ensuring they are properly ligated to prevent bleeding during the thymectomy procedure.
The innominate vein is visible beneath the previously ligated vessel, showing its anatomical position in relation to the secured thymic vessels.
The surgeon carefully dissects the phrenic nerve away from the tumor, ensuring it remains free from any invasion by the tumor.
The surgeon removes the entire pericardial tissue along with the tumor and specimen, ensuring complete excision of the affected area.
The surgeon exposes the right pleura and continues dissection, revealing the formation of the superior vena cava.
The left horn of the thymus is visible, with the innominate vein clearly defined beneath it.
The surgeon carefully dissects the thymic horns as they enter the neck, pulling them down while avoiding bleeding. The superior thymic vessels are identified and ligated to prevent vascular injury.
The surgeon dissects and reflects the entire anterior mediastinal fat, removing it along with the thymectomy specimen.
The posterior thymic vessels, which are direct branches from the arch of the aorta, are visible in the surgical field.
The surgical field shows the floor of the thymus after dissection, with the arch of the aorta and the pulmonary trunk clearly visible.
The phrenic nerve is visible in the surgical field, running along the lateral aspect of the mediastinum, adjacent to the thymus and surrounding structures.
The surgeon extracts the thymectomy specimen through a sub-xiphoid incision, utilizing a wound protector to minimize tissue trauma and avoid rib splitting.
